title: Speculaas
subtitle: Belgian spiced biscuits with cinnamon, clove, and cardamom
excerpt: Thin, crisp Belgian and Dutch spiced biscuits made with dark brown sugar, warm spices, and a touch of bicarbonate of soda. Best rolled to 3 mm for the characteristic snap. Makes about thirty-five.
sourceNotes: Speculaas (Dutch) or speculoos (Belgian) are documented in Low Countries domestic cookbooks from the seventeenth century onward, originally made for the feast of Sint Nikolaas on 5 December. The spice blend of cinnamon, cloves, cardamom, and nutmeg is recorded in multiple public-domain Northern European baking texts. Hannah Glasse, The Art of Cookery (1747), Project Gutenberg: spiced shortbread biscuits with cinnamon and cloves are documented in the Dutch-style biscuit chapter. The USDA public-domain publications on spice uses in baking document the canonical five-spice blend used in Low Countries holiday biscuits.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked and cooled in a tin for up to 3 months.
recipe.batchNotes: Double and bake on two trays.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The dough keeps wrapped in the fridge for 3 days. Keeps baked in an airtight tin for 3 weeks.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, middle shelf; the edges should be a shade darker than the centre, and the top should feel set but not hard when pressed

content.text: Speculaas get their snap from the combination of dark sugar, high bake temperature, and thin rolling. The spice blend is the recipe: cinnamon carries the warmth, cloves give the bite, cardamom the floral note, nutmeg the depth. Roll to 3 mm for the characteristic brittle snap; 5 mm and the centres go soft.

Beat the {{unsalted-butter}} and {{dark-brown-sugar}} together until combined. The mixture won't cream as pale as caster sugar dough; that is correct. Add the {{eggs}} and beat until smooth. Stir together the {{plain-flour}}, {{cinnamon-ground}}, {{ginger-ground}}, {{nutmeg-ground}}, {{cloves}}, {{cardamom-green}}, {{bicarbonate-of-soda}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}}, then add to the butter mixture and work until a smooth, cohesive dough forms. Flatten into a disc, wrap, and refrigerate for at least 1 hour; the dough needs to be firm to roll thinly.

Preheat the oven to 175°C fan. Roll the dough on a lightly floured surface to 3 mm thick. Cut with a 6 cm cutter or a fluted round and space on lined trays. Bake for 10–12 minutes; the edges should be a shade darker than the centre and the surface should feel set when pressed. Leave on the tray for 5 minutes before transferring to a rack. They crisp considerably as they cool.
